risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After completion of baseline assessments, participants were randomized to one of two treatment groups by blocked randomization with stratification on gender. The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: The study explicitly describes a random allocation sequence and blocked/stratified randomization overseen by a statistician, which is sufficient to support that a proper random sequence was generated.

Quote: "After completion of baseline assessments, participants were randomized to one of two treatment groups by blocked randomization with stratification on gender. The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: The paper mentions sealed envelopes and that the sequence came from a statistician independent of participants, but it does not explicitly state that envelopes were sequentially numbered and opaque. Because all 3 SNOSE features are not reported, concealment cannot be confirmed as low risk; there is also no positive evidence that recruiters could foresee assignment, so the appropriate judgement is unclear risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial was technically single-blinded given that patients were aware of which intervention they were assigned to (CRM or HE). However, participants were masked to the research hypothesis and both interventions were presented to participants as health promoting; this design should minimize expectation bias." Quote: "Both interventions were administered... by providers not involved with participant recruitment, data collection, analysis or interpretation... All sessions were taught by the same experienced teacher (KK). Subjects randomized to HE attended the same number, size and frequency of group meetings lead by a professional health educator (LB)." Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants knew their assigned intervention, and the intervention providers necessarily knew which program they were delivering. Because the study includes subjective/self-reported outcomes (e.g. perceived stress, anxiety, depression, anger, hostility, physical activity), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes and behavior; therefore performance bias is high overall despite masking to the study hypothesis.

Quote: "Investigators and the staff collecting the data were blinded to the treatment status of the participants." and "All outcome data were collected in a blinded fashion with respect to the participants' treatment status." Comment: Outcome assessors were research staff/investigators for clinic measures, with automated/lab/imaging systems used for many outcomes (e.g., ultrasound with automatic edge detection, lab assays). Although the study also included subjective patient-reported psychosocial questionnaires, the paper explicitly states that all outcome data were collected blinded to treatment status, which covers the most bias-vulnerable outcomes. Therefore knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "Of the randomized patients, 21 and 23 participants in the CRM and HE groups, respectively, attended the 6-month visit and 19 in each group attended the 12-month visit." Quote: "Data were analyzed following the intention-to-treat principle, i.e. all available data were analyzed on randomized participants (n=68)... using repeated measures mixed effects models... unbiased with unbalanced and missing data, so long as the missing data are non-informative (missing at random)." Comment: Post-randomization outcome data at the main 12-month time point were missing for 14/33 (42.4%) in CRM and 16/35 (45.7%) in HE, totaling 30/68 (44.1%) missing. This exceeds the usual high-risk threshold of about 30% overall attrition. Although loss was balanced between arms and mixed models were used, the paper does not report reasons for dropout or a robustness analysis demonstrating that this very high level of missing data would not bias the outcome estimates.
